title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Troopers say driver drunk in fatal crash near Nenana The Associated Press FAIRBANKS (August 25, 4:15 p.m. ADT) - A Chalkyitsik man was intoxicated when he slammed his car into another vehicle on the Parks Highway near Nenana in July, Alaska State Troopers said. The collision killed Spencer Henry, 32, and his 10-year-old son, Brandon. Five others, including Henry's wife and their two other children, were injured. According to troopers, Henry's blood-alcohol level at the time of the July 2 crash was .29 - more than three times the legal limit. Henry lost control of the car while speeding around a corner, trooper Brian Wassmann said. The car hit a GMC truck driven by Richard Baker, 54, of Fife Lake, Mich. Richard Baker had two fractured femurs. He has undergone numerous surgeries and may …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Child killer sentenced to 10 years MANSLAUGHTER: Kerry Holcomb pleaded no contest in toddler's beating death. By Sheila Toomey Anchorage Daily News (Published: August 14, 2002) A reduced charge in the death of a 2-year-old child earned his killer a 10-year prison sentence Tuesday. Kerry Holcomb, originally indicted for second-degree murder in the June 2000 death of Joey Wolfe, declined to speak on his own behalf before Superior Court Judge Larry Card sentenced him to 15 years for manslaughter, then suspended five of the years. Holcomb pleaded no contest to the charge in April. Card also put Holcomb on probation for 10 years after he is released. Holcomb, 22, was baby-sitting Joey, his girlfriend's son, when the boy said from bed that he needed to go to the bathroom, according to prosec…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Alaska Digest (Published: August 13, 2002) JUNEAU Cruise ship loses propulsion in Southeast A power outage left a Holland America cruise ship temporarily adrift Monday in Lynn Canal in Southeast Alaska. Passengers aboard the Ryndam woke up to views of Auke Bay instead of Glacier Bay after the cruise ship lost power briefly about 1:30 a.m. The ship was directed into Auke Bay until the problem could be fixed, said Lt. Cmdr. Joe Paitl, executive officer at the Coast Guard Marine Safety Office in Juneau. "It's a very serious event anytime you lose power, especially in a ship that size," Paitl said. The Ryndam was on its way from Skagway to Glacier Bay when one of the two generators suddenly stopped running. That caused the ship to lose propulsion, Paitl said. Within a minute the crew had t…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Alaska digest (Published: August 5, 2002) Anchorage Man dies after hitting head on cement wall A 21-year-old man died early Sunday after he fell and hit his head on a cement wall, Anchorage police reported. The man's name was not released pending notification of his family. The young man fell while running down a hill near Tudor Road and Minnesota Drive just before 3 a.m. A witness told police he saw the man fall headfirst into the wall. Police indicated the victim appeared to have been drinking. The man was pronounced dead at Providence Alaska Medical Center. -- Anchorage Daily News Anchorage 4-year-old drowns in Jewel Lake A 4-year-old boy drowned Sunday night while on a family picnic at Jewel Lake Park near 88th Avenue. The child went missing about 6 p.m. Police, family members and …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Federal wildfire dollars went far A good defense (Published: August 2, 2002) Hillside residents are familiar with the sight of yellow-shirted fire crews chopping down dead spruce trees. Federal funds distributed by the Anchorage Fire Department paid the crews. But the money did more. It allowed the city to lease a firefighting helicopter for 45 days in early summer. The helicopter helped with a search-and-rescue mission over Cook Inlet and several brush fires around Anchorage. And volunteer fire departments in Chugiak, Girdwood and Eagle River received new equipment. The city funded wildfire evacuation drills on the Hillside and allowed the city to open temporary wood lots in Eagle River and Anchorage. The city also was able to supply a wood chipper for a time in some Hillside neighbor…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Alaska Ear (Published: July 28, 2002) OVER THE LINE ... Did GOP boss Randy Ruedrich really call the father of soon-to-be-former Rep. Andrew Halcro and complain that Andrew, R-Anchorage, was campaigning door to door with Democrat Ethan Berkowitz? "I was just curious," says Randy. "It might be an issue if he seeks Republican support in the future." Dad reportedly pointed out that Andrew is 37 and makes his own decisions. "At least he didn't call my mother," Halcro said. All this prompts a question: Why is Halcro, who has declined to run for re-election, supporting Berkowitz? He is also throwing a fund-raiser for him. "This has nothing to do with politics," Halcro said. "Ethan was a good friend to me for four years in Juneau." Halcro said he has no plans to change parties. He's also throw…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Voter deadline passes REGISTER Some folks grouse, some say they like new closed primary system as they sign up. By Sean Cockerham Anchorage Daily News (Published: July 29, 2002) Like many other Anchorage residents, Virginia Silver headed to Dimond Center on Sunday. But she was not there to try on shoes or to check out what tops are on sale at The Gap. Silver was there to make darn sure the state of Alaska knows she is a nonpartisan voter. Sunday was the last day to update voter registration for the state primary election on Aug. 27 so the Division of Elections Office in the mall was open. It was important for Silver to double-check on her lack of party affiliation because this election will be the first held under a new state law in which Alaskans will be able to cast ballots only for …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Local folks (Published: July 29, 2002) Eight of 16 first-place winning entries in the Alaska Press Women 2002 Communications Contest (for professional work completed in 2001) received awards during the annual competition of the National Federation of Press Women. Winners were: Judy Koskela, Anchorage, first place for the external annual report she produced for the Girl Scouts Susitna Council; Karen L. Lew, Eagle River, first place for a book about the Harlem Renaissance that she edited, second place for her personal Web site www.KarenLLew.com , and third place for six headlines she wrote as a copy editor for the Anchorage Daily News; Rhonda McBride, Anchorage, first place for her television documentary "Alaska's Silent Epidemic"; Carol Gales, Nome, first place for the four-color newsle…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Murkowski proposal would have cut benefits in villages 'BIZARRE':Senator said even he wouldn't support his amendment. By Liz Ruskin Anchorage Daily News (Published: June 28, 2002) Washington -- Senators generally try to increase the flow of federal money to their states, but Sen. Frank Murkowski filed an amendment to a welfare reform bill that would have reduced allowable benefits in 148 Alaska Native villages. He presented the amendment to the Senate Finance Committee on Wednesday, explaining that his only purpose was to give himself an opportunity to discuss the bad effects of welfare reform in rural Alaska. "Welfare policy has failed to provide incentives for economic growth," he said. "In fact, it has created the opposite." He did not ask the committee to vote on the amendment and …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Youth flight claim wrong, Ulmer says THEME: Thousands leave Alaska seeking jobs, Murkowski says. By Don Hunter Anchorage Daily News (Published: June 6, 2002) Democrat Fran Ulmer's campaign circulated a state labor report Wednesday in an effort to deflate a central theme in Republican Frank Murkowski's economic attack -- that thousands of young adults have fled Alaska in search of jobs. Creating new jobs for a generation of young Alaskans starved for opportunities at home has been a Murkowski campaign banner from the beginning. He raised it recently in a statement issued when he formally filed for office on May 31. "Thirty-five thousand of our young people have left the state over the last eight years to seek better employment opportunities elsewhere," Murkowski said. "This tragic loss …

title: Search articles for...
summary: In Alaska Business News More headlines » Alaska | National | World | Sports Usibelli gets contract to provide coal for military The Associated Press (Published: August 1, 2002) Fairbanks -- The Department of Defense will buy more than $100 million worth of coal from Usibelli Coal Mine during the next five years. The coal will supply Fort Wainwright, Eielson Air Force Base and Clear Air Force Station with power and heat, said Master Sgt. Angel Newman of the Alaska Command in Anchorage. Bill Brophy, vice president of customer relations for Usibelli, said the contract is a continuation of a long-standing relationship with the military. "There's not really a significant change to the volume of the coal," he said. As a result, the contract will not do much to make up for the recent loss of Usib…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Wildfire causes temporary population spike in Medfra INTERIOR: Smokejumpers arrive to help defend village from blaze 2 1/2 miles away. By Joel Gay Anchorage Daily News (Published: August 3, 2002) The Interior village of Medfra more than doubled in size this week when a fire swept out of the black spruce forest to the northeast and eight smokejumpers dropped in to protect a lodge, several homes and the settlement's inhabitants. The North Fork fire started two weeks ago when lightning ignited a blaze 12 miles north of Nikolai and 45 miles east of McGrath. Over the next two weeks, the fire burned 16,000 acres. Hot, dry weather this week fanned the flames, however, and on Thursday the fire leapt into high gear, jumping to nearly 22,000 acres and pushing within 212 miles of Medfra, said Dia…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Line to pay $1.5 million for dumping WASTE Norwegian Line admits lying for three years about illegal discharges. State to investigate cruise line By Catherine Wilson The Associated Press (Published: August 1, 2002) Miami -- The world's fourth-largest cruise line agreed Wednesday to pay a $1.5 million penalty for dumping waste oil and untreated wastewater into the ocean and covering it up with mechanical tricks and false logs. Norwegian Cruise Line Ltd. pleaded guilty to keeping a false logbook in April 2000 and admitted the company lied to the Coast Guard for three years about illegal discharges from its 2,030-passenger Norway liner. "Our oceans are not dumping grounds, and lying to the U.S. Coast Guard will be vigorously prosecuted," Tom Sansonetti, assistant attorney general for the …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Inside Alaska Business (Published: August 1, 2002) Project includes 35 state firms Thirty-five Alaskan-owned businesses are subcontractors on the missile-defense project under construction at Fort Greely near Delta Junction and at Eareckson Air Station on Shemya, according to the Site Activation Command -- Alaska, based at Fort Richardson. The Alaska work is part of a $7.5 billion missile system designed to shoot incoming missiles. The Alaska portion is valued at more than $400 million and is scheduled to be completed in just over two years. The subcontracts to Alaska businesses now exceed $53 million, the command said. The subcontractors include fence companies, surveyors, engineers and construction companies. They also include Fred Meyer and Seekins Ford in Fairbanks, as well as Rent…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | BP urges action on gas pipeline HURRY UP: David Welch warns highway route could lose support. The Associated Press (Published: July 26, 2002) Whitehorse, Yukon -- The three major companies pursuing an Alaska Highway natural gas pipeline could lose interest in the project if they do not see movement toward their goal in the next year or two, according to a top-ranking BP official. David Welch, president of BP Alaska-Canada Gas Pipelines, told Whitehorse reporters that the desire to build a pipeline through Alaska, the Yukon and British Columbia to central Alberta is still very much alive in the hearts of BP, Exxon Mobil and Phillips Petroleum. But without movement in regulatory and fiscal areas by the United States, Canada and Alaska, there could come a day when the interest wanes and t…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Oil chiefs ask for stable tax climate UNCERTAINTY: Executives worry that investors are insecure. By Wesley Loy Anchorage Daily News (Published: July 16, 2002) A cavalcade of oil industry executives on Monday urged the incoming governor and Legislature to erase the looming threat of higher taxes or royalties on oil and gas produced in the state. Yearly worries that the state might seek to solve its mounting revenue shortfalls by extracting more from big oil is threatening continued industry investment in Alaska and could even kill some projects now evolving, the executives said. Charles Pierce, vice president of Unocal Corp., suggested the new governor, to be elected in November, use his or her veto power to freeze oil tax and royalties for four years. That would make oil industry execu…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Kristie Drury shows her mother, Beverly Drury, the lawsuit filed on her behalf Friday morning alleging violations of the Americans With Disabilities Act by Turnagain House restaurant. They met up at a celebration of the act's 12th anniversary Friday on the Delaney Park Strip. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Subsistence Special Session of Alaska Legislature starts May 15 Congress hears subsistence views PRIORITY: Murkowski and Natives disagree in first such hearing in nearly a decade. By Tom Kizzia Anchorage Daily News (Published: May 13, 2002) Should Congress get involved again in Alaska's subsistence debate? Alaska Native leaders and Sen. Frank Murkowski disagreed sharply on that question during a rare congressional oversight hearing on subsistence hunting and fishing Wednesday in Washington, D.C. Both sides said they want the Legislature to adopt a state constitutional amendment allowing a rural subsistence priority. But Murkowski, R-Alaska, said the amendment should be tied to further changes in federal law. Such changes could tighten subsistence definitions and reduce federal oversigh…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | City librarian under fire, announces plans to retire COMPLAINT: Letter by Tim Lynch's staff not related to decision. By Tim Pryor Anchorage Daily News (Published: March 7, 2002) Anchorage City Librarian Tim Lynch has announced plans to retire after some of his employees wrote him a letter complaining about his management. He intends to leave his job in August. Lynch said Monday his decision isn't related to the letter. He said he will be 55 in August and eligible for state retirement. He said he stated his intentions early to give the city time to find a replacement. Mayor George Wuerch appointed Lynch near the end of 2000 after deciding not to keep Moe McGee as head librarian. Since then, Lynch has been caught up in the biggest library controversy in recent years. It began last June w…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Beachcomber encounters rare reptile DISCOVERY: A green sea turtle weighing roughly 150 pounds washes ashore on Admiralty Island. The Associated Press (Published: December 1, 2001) Juneau -- Kirt Marsh of Petersburg was walking along a rocky beach on southern Admiralty Island recently when he came upon a very odd rock. It was 3 feet long and 2 feet wide -- huge compared to the other rocks on the beach. Then he saw a head. "I looked at it and realized it was a turtle," said Marsh, a commercial fisherman who had never seen a sea turtle here in 20 years of plying Alaska waters. The turtle was dead, though. The creature was identified by the state Department of Fish and Game in Petersburg as a green turtle. That's a very unusual find in Alaska, said Mike Payne of the National Marine Fisheri…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Disaster aid eyed for Dyea LANDSLIDE Skagway faces task of clearing debris from creek, property. The Associated Press (Published: August 1, 2002) Juneau -- Skagway city officials assessing damage from a landslide and flood that forced the evacuation of nearby Dyea plan to ask for state assistance. Thousands of cubic feet of mud and debris from the July 23 landslide need to be removed from West Creek and the city plans to ask Gov. Tony Knowles for a disaster declaration, said Bob Ward, Skagway city manager. Ward is also compiling information about flood damage to property owners to make a disaster declaration request to the state, he said. Dyea is a small mining town with about 25 year-round residents and many summer residents about 10 miles north of Skagway. Residents were forced to ev…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Community datebook (Published: August 1, 2002) BLOOD BANK Blood Bank of Alaska has ongoing needs. Donations can be made 10 a.m.-6 p.m. Monday through Saturday at 4000 Laurel St. Appointments are recommended at 563-3110 in Anchorage, 1-907-260-5672 in Soldotna and 1-907-376-1195 in Wasilla. Current needs include: Condition Red (one-day supply only): O-negative Condition Yellow (two-day supply): A-negative Condition Green (three-day or more supply): O-positive, A-positive, B-positive, B-negative, AB-positive, AB-negative TODAY COMMUNITY Alaska Press Women luncheon, featuring an opportunity to network, 11:30 a.m., Golden Lion Hotel, 36th Avenue and Seward Highway -- $15 members, $16 guests, $6 for coffee and dessert only. Call to inquire about reservations, 274-4723. Anchorage Garden Club…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Even mosquitoes have defenders PESTS 'They're part of a large food chain,' experts report. By S.J. Komarnitsky Anchorage Daily News (Published: July 29, 2002) It's part of summer life in Alaska: the tinny buzzing, the piercing bites, and itchy welts on arms, legs and faces. And so is the dream of purging neighborhoods of that poking pest, the mosquito. Alaskans have waged a futile war with open palm, smelly smoke and pesticides. The village of Larsen Bay on Kodiak recently proposed debugging the town with Mosquito Magnets, machines that suck biting insects to a mummylike death in a dehydrating net. But lost in these visions of mass extinction is thought of what might happen if we actually succeeded in ridding our state of skeeters. In other words, are mosquitoes good for anything? Lots…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Three-day Inuit conference kicks off Thursday at UAA NEW IDEA: More Native participation in research is being sought. By Tom Kizzia Anchorage Daily News (Published: July 29, 2002) When Gordon Pullar brought some Alaska Native university students to Scotland two years ago for the world's leading academic conference on Inuit and Eskimo societies, everyone in the group was struck by the relative absence of one group: the Inuit people themselves. This week, when the biannual Inuit Studies Conference convenes in Anchorage, things will be different. Many speakers and programs at the three-day conference will address the fast-spreading notion that Native peoples of the Arctic should be collaborators in research, not merely subjects. Much of the change is due to Pullar and his students from th…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | River watchers Alaska Natives join biologists in learning more about Kuskokwim fish By Joel Gay Anchorage Daily News (Published: July 28, 2002) Carlos Monsivais, left, and Frank Burke release a salmon after measuring and tagging it while working a shift on a Birch Crossing fish wheel earlier this month on the Kuskokwim River between Aniak and Kalskag. Two fish wheels were operating on the north and south banks of the river. (Photo by Erik Hill / Anchorage Daily News) Crews studying the Kuskokwim salmon runs this month are measuring the length of the fish from the center of the eye to the fork of the tail. State fisheries managers knew little about the river's salmon until the early 1990s. (Photo by Erik Hill / Anchorage Daily News) The Tuluksak River weir, set up about 10 years ago, al…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Students' test scores rise PROBLEMS: Math scores dip between grades; gap exists with Native students. By Katie Pesznecker Anchorage Daily News (Published: July 26, 2002) Alaska students in third, sixth and eighth grades showed improved scores overall on last March's round of state-required benchmark tests in reading, writing and math. But the decline in math scores between third and eighth grade remains, as does a large gap separating Alaska Native students' scores from their white classmates' higher marks. This was the third year students took the benchmarks. The tests function as academic thermometers as kids trudge toward the high school graduation qualifying exam, which is taken in 10th grade. Students graduating in 2004 or later must pass that exam to graduate. Shirley Holloway, s…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Panel OKs Shishmaref move money STEVENS: Senator also seeking funding for additional projects in the state. By Liz Ruskin Washington Bureau (Published: July 25, 2002) Washington -- The village of Shishmaref, while losing ground to the Chuckchi Sea, has gained a toehold in the federal budget. A spending bill cleared the Senate Appropriations Committee on Wednesday with $1 million for an environmental impact study on moving the community of 600 to more protected ground. The village voted overwhelmingly Saturday to relocate. Storms have undercut permafrost and chewed the coastline of the barrier island on which Shishmaref sits, about 150 miles north of Nome. "By golly, that's encouraging," said Percy Nayokpuk, president of the Shishmaref Native Corp., when told of the bill. The money, if …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Ross says he would prefer woman as running mate CAMPAIGN 'If I had my druthers, I'd have to say I'd go for Sarah Palin.' By Don Hunter Anchorage Daily News (Published: July 25, 2002) Wayne Anthony Ross took his campaign for the Republican nomination for governor to the friendly territory of a conservative talk show on KENI radio Wednesday morning. He was asked which of the five Republican primary candidates for lieutenant governor he hoped voters would pair him with in the general election. Ross, an Anchorage attorney making his second bid for governor, allowed that a ticket pairing him with another "old guy" like Robin Taylor or Loren Leman might be fun. But, he said, "I'd like to see a woman as lieutenant governor to balance the ticket." He went on to say he thought the second spot w…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Ross invites Murkowski to 'come home' DEBATE: Senator's leading GOP gubernatorial opponent sends letter of challenge. By Don Hunter Anchorage Daily News (Published: July 23, 2002) Sen. Frank Murkowski's leading Republican primary opponent in the race for governor says he should return to Alaska, debate his opponents and take stands on the major issues. "If you really want to run for governor of Alaska, Frank, then I respectfully suggest that it is time for you to 'come home' and be a candidate for governor," candidate Wayne Anthony Ross said in a letter sent to Murkowski this weekend. Ross is an Anchorage attorney who drew more than 17,000 votes in a three-way race in the 1998 Republican governor primary. His letter was prompted by Murkowski's earlier proposal that he and the likely De…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Bears come out for climbers scaling Mount Rumble Shawn Lyons Hiking & Climbing (Published: July 28, 2002) Hey! Jim Sayler yelled into the valley. Despite being near the roaring waters of Peters Creek, the massive brown head of a bear swiveled and peered up at the sound. She heard you I gasped. For a moment, we were motionless in an immense amphitheater of snow-covered peaks. Then the bear ran toward the creek, with two black dots nearby. Crashing across the frothing 20-foot wide glacial stream, she turned and waited. But her two cubs, reluctant to brave the waters, did not emerge from the willow. Plowing back across the river, the sow tried again. Again, the two cubs stayed put. So for a third time, the sow crossed the creek. This time, one of the cubs followed its mother, leaving a st…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Murkowski, Ulmer ready to debate ELECTION: U.S. senator asks lieutenant governor for cooperation in scheduling. By Don Hunter Anchorage Daily News (Published: July 17, 2002) Saying important Senate business will keep him in Washington, D.C., for most of the fall campaign for governor, Republican Sen. Frank Murkowski on Tuesday asked his likely Democratic opponent, Lt. Gov. Fran Ulmer, to help him make the most of his limited time in Alaska. In a letter to Ulmer, Murkowski says their campaigns should cooperate in planning a series of debates leading up to the Nov. 5 general election. Ulmer's campaign says she's ready for that. "In the interest of making such debates meaningful and accessible to the majority of the public, I would appreciate your help in working out a schedule to accommo…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Caribou country Hunters discover herd walks right through camp By Bob Mottram Tacoma News Tribune (Published: July 6, 2002) Caribou numbers in many areas of Alaska are high. Those who hunt in remote areas have an excellent chance of success. This hunter looks over a small band of caribou near the head of the Mulchatna River. (Photo by Bob Robb) Thousands of hunters eagerly await the annual list of permit winners to see if they get the chance to hunt one of Alaska's many caribou. (Photo by Bob Hallinen / Anchorage Daily News) Click on photo to enlarge MULCHATNA RIVER COUNTRY -- John McGlenn crawled out of his sleeping bag while first light was nothing but a promise, rustled around inside the tent getting into his clothes, then unzipped the tent door and stepped outside. I lingered in my…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Hard to hunt Finding, taking a grizzly is not an easy task By Bob Robb Daily News Correspondent (Published: July 6, 2002) (Photo by Bob Robb) Click on photo to enlarge A friend and noted Alaska big-game guide and I were discussing brown-bear hunting one day. After I told him of my frustrations at not being able to find any bears, his comment was the most apropos I have heard on the topic. He said, simply, "Even when there are a lot of 'em, there ain't many of 'em." How true. Despite the fact that brown-bear populations have grown throughout most of Alaska, locating a good bear can be as difficult as finding a four-leaf clover. For most sportsmen, brown-bear hunting consists of applying for a coveted and strictly limited Kodiak Island permit. If one is not drawn, one doesn't hunt brown …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Hunt smarter, not harder They may be big but finding moose can test any hunter's mettle By Bob Robb Daily News Correspondent (Published: July 6, 2002) (Photo by Bob Hallinen / Anchorage Daily News) (Photo by Jim Lavrakas / Anchorage Daily News) Click on photo to enlarge In 1939, on the eve of World War II, Winston Churchill told the world, "I cannot forecast to you the action of Russia. It is a riddle wrapped in a mystery inside an enigma." He may just as well have been speaking about Alaska's moose, an animal so huge most inexperienced hunters think finding one to shoot should be a snap. Yet a quick look at the miserly statewide success rates paint a different picture. In truth, finding a moose for the freezer is hard work, while dealing with a moose after the shot can be a backbreaki…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Dipnetting delight Thousands flock to Kenai River to stock freezers By Craig Medred Anchorage Daily News (Published: May 16, 2002) A dipnetter hauls a red salmon to shore at the mouth of the Kenai River. New regulations will limit the hours dipnetters can fish in 2002. (Erik Hill / Anchorage Daily News) Click on photo to enlarge KENAI -- Along the long sand beaches behind the grassy dunes at the mouth of the Kenai River, the American melting pot was on full display. Koreans, Samoans, Filipinos, African-Americans, Indians, Native Americans and all variety of white folk, rich and poor, prowled the shoreline in the grayness of an Alaska night. The first man I met on the beach drawled two words: "They're coming." Then, smiling, he disappeared into the gloom of the night toward the cul-de-s…

title: Fish fever is difficult to combat
summary: Willow Creek -- The slack-water channel at the Susitna River confluence looked like a fish farm's holding pond Thursday. Tens of dozens of pink and chum salmon, along with the occasional silver, milled in big circles between the grass banks. Anglers stood by and drooled. Catching Pacific salmon in still water -- or, more correctly, catching them legally -- is one of the greatest challenges of Alaska angling. That didn't seem to discourage a couple dozen afternoon anglers who hit the water just a short walk from the state campground. Most appeared to be tourists. A few seemed to recognize that the best fishing would be found along the edge of the mixing line between the fast-flowing Susitna and the clear water trickling out the southernmost outlet of this creek. Many others, however, found …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Counterfeiters cash in with phony state checks SCHEME: Businesses that accepted the bogus checks have lost $27,000. By Nicole Tsong Anchorage Daily News (Published: August 3, 2002) Several bogus state checks have surfaced in Anchorage in the past two days, and by Friday, someone had gotten away with almost $27,000 in cash, the state Department of Revenue said. Five phony checks were discovered Thursday by KeyBank, which handles the state account. Another three were cashed the next morning, said Larry Persily, deputy commissioner of revenue. "This is the first time anyone here can remember running into counterfeit checks," he said. The checks are labeled "State of Alaska Treasury Warrant General Fund" and look similar to the genuine thing. They are not payroll checks. Made out to four d…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Home sales remain on track for another strong year (Published: July 28, 2002) Anchorage's residential real estate market is experiencing yet another strong year. Last year was an excellent year for the market and this year appears to be more of the same. Even though 2002 has not statistically surpassed 2001 in all areas, the residential market continues to be good for buyers and sellers. The following chart shows interesting comparisons in residential real estate for the last three years. The first is the average monthly original list price (AvgOLP) compared to the average sale price (AvgSP). This chart also shows how the original listing price typically parallels the sales prices. An interesting note is the crossing of these lines in March and April 2002. This was an extremely active …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Alaska digest (Published: August 3, 2002) AMBLER 12-year-old swimming with friends in Kobuk River drowns A 12-year-old Ambler boy drowned in the Kobuk River on Wednesday night. Alaska State Troopers spokesman Greg Wilkinson said Dennis Cleveland was swimming with friends in the Kobuk River when he apparently swam too far from shore and could not get back. He disappeared about 6 p.m. and his body was recovered at 7:15 p.m. Rescuers attempted cardiopulmonary resuscitation for about 45 minutes before the boy was declared dead. Ambler is 325 miles northwest of Fairbanks. -- The Associated Press JUNEAU Woman killed when she loses control of her motorcycle A Juneau woman was killed on Wednesday after she lost control of her motorcycle and struck a guardrail. Erica Swiatkowski, 19, was travel…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Lead level a concern at school firing ranges Daily News Staff (Published: August 3, 2002) State health officials are recommending that schools evaluate their firing ranges for lead exposure problems after members of a rural target-shooting program had higher than normal blood levels of lead. The state Section of Epidemiology released a report this week about a January investigation into a school's indoor firing range. Dr. John Middaugh, state epidemiologist, did not name the school in Alaska's Interior, but did say he believed the indoor shooting range was still closed. Indoor shooting ranges can accumulate lead several ways, Middaugh said. When gunpowder ignites to propel a bullet, the heat turns the lead into a gas that enters the air. A fast-moving bullet traveling down the barrel a…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Officer shoots, kills Wainwright soldier CHASE: Police say the man rammed into police cars. The Associated Press (Published: July 29, 2002) Fairbanks -- A soldier from Fort Wainwright was shot and killed by a Fairbanks police officer after a high-speed chase through the city. Michael S. Eutenier, 19, of Kelseyville, Calif., died Saturday at Alaska Regional Medical Center, a nursing supervisor said. He was placed on life support until his family arrived. Eutenier and a friend, Derrick A. Lawhorn, 22, had attended a barbecue at Chena Lakes Recreation Area on Saturday evening with friends, according to Lawhorn's girlfriend. The 20-year-old Fairbanks woman said the two men began to taunt her by calling her names. She said one of them took her sandals and threw them into the campfire. At on…
